Facts.text: Solar eclipse (2m 07s) seen at Ugarit by Mesopotamian astronomers "On the day of the new moon, in the month of Hiyar, the Sun was put to shame, and went down in the daytime, with Mars in attendance."
Maya ruler 18 Rabbit of Copán is captured and beheaded by his former vassal Cauac Sky ruler of the nearby city of Quiriguá
John II becomes Duke of Brabant and Limburg
Count Hartmann II becomes the ruler of Vaduz, Liechtenstein
Battle of Beverhoutsveld near Bruges in modern-day Belgium - the army of Ghent beats a drunken Bruges militia
Kongo monarch Nkuwu Nzinga is baptized by Portuguese missionaries, adopting the baptismal name of João I
Strategic city of Santa Cruz de Mompox founded on the Magdalena River (modern Colombia) by Spanish conquistadors
Ferdinand I officially appointed Holy Roman Emperor after his brother Charles abdicated in 1556
Treaty of Loudun ends French civil war
Spanish silver fleet sails to Panama
French huguenot leader Duke De Rohan signs accord with Spain
Bridge at Rowley, Massachusetts begins charging tolls for animals
Sweden, Poland, Brandenburg, and Austria sign the Treaty of Oliva, one of the peace treaties ending the Second Northern War
Johannes Hevelius observes 3rd transit of Mercury ever to be seen
Royal charter granted to Connecticut
French conquering fleet at Curacao, 1200 die
Pierre de Marivaux's play "La Double Inconstance" premieres in Paris
William IV Prince of Orange appointed Stadtholder of Holland and Utrecht
1st North American medical college opens in Philadelphia
Constitution of May 3 is proclaimed by the Great Sejm (Parliament) of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, the first modern constitution in Europe
Washington, D.C. is incorporated as a city
Finnish War: Sweden loses the fortress of Sveaborg to Russia
Peninsular War: The Madrid rebels who rose up on May 2 are fired upon near Príncipe Pío hill
Battle at Tolentino: Austria beats King Joachim of Naples
Society for the Propagation of the Faith founded in Lyon, France
First regular steam train passenger service in the US starts in South Carolina, with U.S.-built locomotive “The Best Friend of Charleston”
The University of Athens is founded by King Otto of Greece - first modern university in the Eastern Mediterranean
New Zealand is proclaimed a colony independent of New South Wales
1st African American lawyer, Macon B. Allen, admitted to the bar in Massachusetts
Fire kills 1,600 in a popular theater in Canton, China
Mexican Army begins the Siege of Fort Texas near Brownsville, Texas, during the Mexican–American War
The May Uprising in Dresden begins - the last of the German revolutions of 1848
Sixth major fire in San Francisco destroys 1500-2000 buildings
Antwerp-Rotterdam railway opens
Charles XV of Sweden-Norway is crowned king of Sweden
General Winfield Scott presents his Anaconda Plan for the North against the South in American Civil War
Lincoln asks for 42,000 Army Volunteers and another 18,000 seamen
Battle of Salem Church, Virginia
The Second Battle of Fredericksburg occurs in Virginia, part of the Chancellorsville Campaign of the American Civil War
3rd day in Battle at Alexandria Louisiana: Confederate assault
Hudson's Bay Company gives up all claims to Vancouver Island
M. A. Maclean elected 1st mayor of Vancouver, British Columbia
Nanaimo Mine Disaster: explosion in the mine kills 150 men - British Columbia's worst mine disaster
Camp Merriman forms at Presidio San Francisco (see 0517)
Fire destroys 1,700 buildings in Jacksonville, Florida
British-controlled Egypt takes Sinai peninsula from Turkey
Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States is renamed the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA)
Irish Nationalists Patrick Pearse, Thomas MacDonagh and Thomas Clarke are executed by firing squad following their involvement in the Easter Rising
Afghanistan Emir Amanoellah begins war against Great Britain
Northern Ireland is created under the UK Government of Ireland Act partitioning off six north eastern counties with a Protestant majority
West Virginia imposes first state sales tax
Mayor Hylan closes 2 streets for building of Yankee Stadium in the Bronx, NYC
Salt layer find at Winterswijk
First non-stop North American transcontinental flight (NY-San Diego) is completed
Aleph Zadik Aleph is formed in Omaha, Nebraska by Sam Beber
Pulitzer prize awarded to Sinclair Lewis (Arrowsmith)
US Marines land in Nicaragua (9 months after leaving), stay until 1933
Japanese atrocities in Jinan, China
Prussia bans anti-fascists
24 tourists begin 1st air-charter holiday from London, England to Basel, Switzerland
Nellie T. Ross takes office as the first female director of the US Mint
French People's Front win elections
Concentration camp at Flossenburg goes into use
Lefty Grove defeats Tigers 4-3 for 1st of record 20 consecutive wins at his home field Fenway Park; he doesn't lose there until May 12 1941
The All India Forward Bloc is formed by Subhash Chandra Bose
German air raid on Liverpool, England
WPAT-AM radio in Patterson, New Jersey begins broadcasting
German Luftwaffe again bombs Exeter, destroying its town centre
Japanese troop attack Tulagi, Gavutu & Tanambogo, Solomon Islands during WWII
Nazis execute 71 Dutch resistance fighters in reprisal at Sachsenhausen concentration camp in Oranienburg, Germany
Nazis require Dutch Jews to wear a Jewish star
Strike against obligatory labor camps ends, after 200 killed
US 1st armour division occupies Mateur, Tunisia
Meat rationing ends in US
1st Polish Armoured Division of the Polish Armed Forces in the West occupies Wilhelmshafen in Germany
German ocean liner SS Cap Arcona, laden with prisoners, is sunk by the Royal Air Force in the western Baltic Sea, killing 5,800 people in one of the largest maritime losses of life in World War II
Louis de Visser, Dutch Communist politician (CPN), killed in Allied bombing of German prison ship SS Cap Arcona at 66
International military tribunal in Tokyo begins
1st broadcast of "CBS Evening News" - longest running network news show in the US
1st firing of a US Viking rocket; reached 80 km
Gil McDougald ties major league record with 6 RBIs in 1 inning
NY Yankee Gil McDougald is 5th to get 6 RBIs in an inning (9th)
Royal Festival Hall opens in London
The Festival of Britain opens
First airplane lands at the geographic North Pole
Westchester conference of American Library Association proclaims "Freedom to Read"
WTVO TV channel 17 in Rockford, IL (NBC) begins broadcasting
KTEN TV channel 10 in Ada-Ardmore, Oklahoma(ABC) begins broadcasting
WHA TV channel 21 in Madison, Wisconsin (PBS) begins broadcasting
A new range of mountains is discovered in Antarctica, with two peaks over 13,000 feet
WINS suspends disc jockey Alan Freed for being charged with inciting a riot at a Boston concert, he quits, charges are dropped
Tiger's Charlie Maxwell hits 4 consecutive HRs in a doubleheader
Musical "The Fantasticks" by Harvey Schmidt and Tom Jones opens in Greenwich Village, NYC, becomes “the longest-running musical in the universe”
Express train crashes into the wreckage of a commuter train and a freight train, killing 163 and injuring 400 in Tokyo, Japan
Birmingham officials turn high pressure hoses and dogs on children's crusade protest against segregation prompting widespread condemnation
Leslie Narum is only Baltimore Oriole to homer on his 1st at bat
Cambodia drops diplomatic relations with the US
First use of satellite TV, Today Show on Early Bird satellite
KTCI TV channel 17 in St. Paul-Minneapolis, MN (PBS) 1st broadcast
Pulitzer prize awarded to Irwin Unger (Greenback Era)
WDHO (now WNWO) TV channel 24 in Toledo, OH (ABC) begins broadcasting
African American students seize finance building at Northwestern University
Holland Pirate Radio Station VRON becomes Radio Veronica Intl
Trans-Arabian Pipeline delivery from Saudi Arabia to the Mediterranean interrupted in Syria, driving oil tanker rates to all time highs
World premiere of The Beatles' "Let it Be" documentary film directed by Michael Lindsay-Hogg, in New York City
Pulitzer prize  for General Non-Fiction awarded to John Toland for "The Rising Sun"
US National Public Radio begins programming with newscast "All Things Considered" airing on 112 stations nationwide
US President Nixon administration authorizes arrest of 13,000 anti-war protesters in 3 days
Chicago's Sears Tower, world's tallest building (443 m), tops out
Christa Vahlensieck runs female world record marathon (2:40:15.8)
Panama 747SP lands after record flight around world (46:26)
1977 NFL Draft: Ricky Bell from USC first pick by Tampa Bay Buccaneers
"Sun Day" - solar energy events held in the US
First unsolicited bulk commercial email ("spam") is sent by a Digital Equipment Corporation marketing representative to every ARPANET address on the US West Coast
Last cricket test match appearance for Bobby Simpson, at Kingston
West Indies all set to lose the cricket test v Australia at Kingston till riots end game
1979 NFL Draft: Tom Cousineau from Ohio State first pick by Buffalo Bills
Martin Sherman's play "Bent" premieres in London
"Moony, Shapiro Songbook" opens & closes at Morosco Theater NYC
ABC's All Talk network begins on radio (2 west coast stations)
NY Times reports that military will get 25% of NASA's budget
US bishops condemn nuclear weapons
Date of $5 million check in "A View To A Kill"
Bomb explodes aboard Air Lanka Flight 512 on the ground at Colombo airport, killing 21
Cubs 3rd baseman Ron Cey hits his 300th & 301st HR
NASA launches Goes-G, it fails to achieve orbit
Miami Herald reports a woman (Donna Rice) spent Friday and Saturday with presidential candidate Gary Hart creating a media frenzy that forces him to withdraw from the race
4,200 kg Colombian cocaine in seized at Tarpon Springs Florida
Jasper Johns' painting "Diver" sells for $4,200,000
The Declaration of Windhoek (on press freedom) signed by African journalists
Baltimore Orioles Gregg Olson, 25, is youngest to record 100 saves
Ohio Glory wins 1st WLAF game (after 6 loses), beat Frankfurt 20-17
D66/Dutch Liberal Party win Dutch 2nd Parliamentary election
Australia beat West Indies to regain the Frank Worrell Cricket Trophy
David Bell debuts for the Indians (3rd generation player, Gus & Buddy)
Martin Moxon and Michael Vaughan make 362 for the 1st wicket, Yorkshire v Glamorgan
Oklahoma City, Oklahoma is slammed by an F5 tornado killing forty-two people, injuring 665, and causing $1 billion in damage. The tornado was one of 66 from the 1999 Oklahoma tornado outbreak.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 11,000 for the first time in its history at 11,014.70.
World Snooker Championship, Crucible Theatre, Sheffield: Stephen Hendry of Scotland defeats Welshman Mark Williams 18-11 for a record seventh world crown
Sport of geocaching begins with the first cache placed and the GPS coordinates posted on Usenet
The United States loses its seat on the UN Human Rights Commission for the first time since the commission was formed in 1947
A military MiG-21 aircraft crashes into the Bank of Rajasthan in India, killing eight.
New Hampshire's famous Old Man of the Mountain collapses
Al-Qaeda member Zacarias Moussaoui is sentenced to life in prison for his part in the 9/11 attacks, in Alexandria, Virginia
Armavia Flight 967 crashes into the Black Sea, killing 113 people on board, with no survivors
British girl Madeleine McCann disappears from her bed in a holiday apartment in Praia da Luz, Portugal
Aorun zhaoi, a Theropod dinosaur, dating from 161 million years ago, is discovered in China
Chelsea wins the 2014–15 English football Premier League
Atlanta Falcons quarterback Matt Ryan becomes NFL's first $30m per season player after agreeing to $150m contract extension
Indian government confirms around 110 people have died in dust storms in Rajasthan and Uttar Pradesh in the last 9 days
Kilauea volcano on Big Island, Hawaii begins erupting forcing the evacuation of Hawaii Volcanoes National Park and residential areas
Cyclone Fani strikes Odisha State, India and Bangladesh, killing 33 people after one of the biggest human evacuations ever - 1.2 million in India in 24 hours
Brazilian confirmed COVID-19 cases pass 100,000 with the death toll at 7,025, the 9th country to do so
DR Congo announces the end of the latest Ebola outbreak after three months with a death toll of six
Former U.S. Senator and astronaut Bill Nelson, becomes Administrator of NASA
German police announce operation and arrests into 'Boystown' world's largest child abuse image website with 400,000 members worldwide
Subway overpass carrying a train collapses in Mexico City killing at least 24 people
US Environmental Protection Agency takes first significant step against climate change by announcing new limits on hydrofluorocarbons (-85% over 15 years)
World Snooker Championship, Crucible Theatre, Sheffield: Englishman Mark Selby wins his 4th world title with an 18-15 win over countryman Shaun Murphy
Heatwave continues across North and Central India disproportionally affecting the poor, with March and April temperatures the hottest ever recorded in 122 years
US government says W.N.B.A. player Brittney Griner has been "wrongfully detained" in Russia, after being taken into custody when drugs were found in her luggage in Feb 2022
13-year-old Serbian student shoots and kills nine people at his Belgrade school, eight students and a security guard, before handing himself in
Liberal Leader Peter Dutton becomes the first Leader of the Opposition to lose his seat in Australian electoral history, following his loss toAnthony Albanese
People's Action Party, led by Lawrence Wong, wins re-election in a landslide victory in Singapore's general election
PopeJulius IIopens the Fifth Council of Lateran at St. John Lateran Basilica in Rome

After confessing to corruption, Lord Chancellor of EnglandFrancis Baconis sentenced to imprisonment in the Tower of London, a large £40,000 fine and banishment from court, Parliament and public office
English Upper house accepts Act of Attainder to execute Thomas Wentworth, 1st Earl of Strafford, a leading advisor to Charles I
Edmond Halleyobserves total eclipse phenomenon "Baily's Beads"
English poetLord Byronswims across the dangerous Hellespont Strait in Turkey (modern day Dardanelles)
Canadian poet and physicianJohn McCraewrites the poem "In Flanders Fields" at Ypres on the Western Front
Ernest Bloch's "Schelomo: Rhapsodie Hébraïque" and "Israel Symphony" premiere in Carnegie Hall, NYC, with Artur Bodanzky conducting the former, and the composer conducting the latter
NY YankeeJoe DiMaggiomakes his major-league debut, gets 3 hits
Margaret Mitchellwins the Pulitzer Prize for her novel "Gone with the Wind"
Pulitzer prize awarded toUpton Sinclairfor his novel "Dragon's Teeth" about the Nazi rise to power
"Going My Way," directed by Leo McCarey and starringBing Crosby, premieres in New York (Academy Award for Best Picture, 1945)
Allies arrest German physicistWerner Heisenberg
Japan's post-war constitution goes into effect, granting universal suffrage, strippingEmperor Hirohitoof all but symbolic power and outlawing Japan's right to make war
Pulitzer prize awarded to James Michener for his novel "Tales of the South Pacific" andTennessee Williamsfor his play "A Streetcar Named Desire"
Pulitzer prizes awarded toCharles Lindberghfor his biography "The Spirit of St. Louis" and John Patrick for drama for his play "The Teahouse of August Moon"
TheAnne FrankHouse opens in Amsterdam, The Netherlands
Erich HoneckersucceedsWalter Ulbrichtas East German communist party leader
Pulitzer prize for Fiction awarded toSaul Bellowfor "Humboldt's Gift"
MLB Cleveland IndianBobby Bondshits his 300th HR (2nd to have 300 HRs & 300 stolen bases)
Giants 1st basemanWillie McCoveyhits his 521st & final HR
Texas RangerFerguson Jenkinsbecomes 4th to win 100 games in AL & NL
President Reaganbegins 5 minute weekly radio broadcasts
Soviet leaderYuri Andropovdecreases nuclear weapons in Europe
New York YankeesDon Mattinglyis 6th to hit 3 sacrifice flies in a game
356th & final episode of CBS second longest running series TV show "Dallas", (2nd only to "Gunsmoke")
Future Hockey HOF right wingCam Neelyinjures knee on a cheap shot by Ulf Samuelsson in Wales Conference Finals; injury limits him to only 162 games for remainder of NHL career; retires at 31
NY MetEddie Murrayis 24th to hit 400 HRs
Russian chess grandmasterGarry Kasparovbegins chess match with IBM supercomputer Deep Blue
"Spider-Man", starringTobey Maguireas Peter Parker/Spider-Man and Willem Dafoe as Norman Osborn/The Green Goblin, premieres
Fall Out Boy's breakthrough album "From Under the Cork Tree" is released through Island Records
The Palmetto Playground in Brooklyn is renamed afterBeastie Boys' rapperAdam Yauch
Chris Isaakis confirmed as a judge on the seventh season of "The X Factor Australia"
Ted Cruzsuspends his campaign to be the Republican Presidential nominee
Academy of Motion Pictures Arts and Sciences members vote to expelBill CosbyandRoman Polanski
Bollywood actors both posthumously recognized at India's National Film Awards;Sridevi KapoorBest Actress for "Mum" and Vinod Khanna receives Dadasaheb Phalke award.
InvestorWarren Buffettdumps his holdings in four major US airlines saying "the world has changed" for aviation, reflecting an increasingly bleak outlook for the industry
US Secretary of StateMike Pompeosays China responsible for the spread and severity of COVID-19 and should be held accountable
Ajay Banga is elected President of the World Bank after being nominated by US PresidentJoe Biden
Manchester City's Norwegian strikerErling Haalandscores in a 3-0 win over West Ham to set a new record for most goals scored in a single Premier League season (35)
MLB LA Angels pitcherShohei Ohtanijoins NY Yankee legend Babe Ruth as only pitcher to strike out 500 batters and hit over 100 career home runs in 6-4 over the Cardinals in St. Louis

Mysterious explosive drone attack on the Kremlin in Moscow has no clear motive or link to people behind it, despite Russian claim it was attempt to assassinateVladimir Putin, amid Ukrainian and US denials
American swimmerKatie Ledeckybreaks her own world record for the 6th time in the 800-meter freestyle event at the TYR Pro Swim Series in Fort Lauderdale, Florida
Labor government led byAnthony Albanese, wins re-election in landslide victory in Australian federal election

Lady Gaga's free concert at Copacabana Beach in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, draws crowd estimated at 2.5 million attendees, the largest concert in history for a female artist; police later reveal they thwarted a bombing attempt
Day depicted by Spanish painterFrancisco Goyain his "The Third of May," which he paints in 1814
Britain's Trade Union Congress calls for the country's first-ever general strike, begins at 1 minute to midnight in support of striking coal miners and lasts 9 days
Constantine III, Byzantine Emperor from February to May 641, born in Constantinople (d. 641)
Cecily Neville, mother of Edward IV of England and Richard III of England, born in Raby Castle, Durham, England (d. 1495)
Pedro González de Mendoza, Spanish cardinal and statesman, born in Guadalajara, Spain (d. 1495)
Margaret of York [Margaret of Burgundy], English wife of Charles I, Duke of Burgundy, born in Fotheringhay Castle, Northamptonshire, England (d. 1503)
Bartholomaeus of Braga [Fernandez], Dominican theologian and Archbishop Emeritus of Braga, born in Mártires, Santa Maria Maior, Lisbon (d. 1590)
Joannes Antonides van der Goes, Dutch poet (Bellone aen bant), born in Goes, Netherlands (d. 1684)
Johann Valentin Meder, German composer, born in Free Imperial City of Erfurt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1719)
Matthäus Daniel Pöppelmann, German architect, born in Herford, Germany (d. 1736)
Jan Jacob Mauricius, Dutch governor-general of Suriname (1742-51), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1768)
Henri Pitot, Italian-French engineer, born in Aramon, France (d. 1771)
Johann Adolph Scheibe, German music theorist and composer, born in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1776)
Alexis Clairaut, French mathematician (Clairaut's theorem), born in Paris (d. 1765)
Florian Leopold Gassmann, Bohemian composer, born in Brüx, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1774)
Friedrich Schwindl, Dutch composer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1786)
Friedrich Wilhelm Weis, German physician and composer, born in Göttingen, Electorate of Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1826)
Braz Francisco de Lima, Portuguese composer, organist, mestre de capela, and pedagogue, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1813)
August von Kotzebue, German dramatist, born in Weimar, Germany (d. 1819)
Élisabeth of France, princess of France, youngest sister of king Louis XVI, born in Versailles, France (d. 1794)
Charles Tennant, Scottish chemist and industrialist, born in Laigh Corton, Alloway, Ayrshire, Scotland (d. 1838)
Giuseppe Acerbi, Italian traveller and naturalist, born in Castel Goffredo, Milan, Italy (d. 1846)
José de la Riva-Agüero, Peruvian soldier and politician, 1st President of Peru (1823), born in Lima, Peru (d. 1858)
Vissarion Belinsky, Russian literary critic (Sovremennik) and influential Westernizer, born in Sveaborg, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1848)
Hermanus Willem Witteveen, Dutch theologist, born in Boornbergum, Netherlands (d. 1884)
Montgomery Cunningham Meigs, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1892)
Nicola De Giosa, Italian composer, born in Bari, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1885)
Charles XV, King of Sweden and Norway (1859-72), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1872)
Édouard Drumont, French anti-semitic journalist, born in Paris (d. 1917)
Richard D'Oyly Carte, English opera impresario (Gilbert & Sullivan operas), born in London (d. 1901)
Thomas Kelly, Irish-Australian cricket batsman (2 Tests, 1877-79; HS 35; Victoria CA), born in County Waterford, Ireland (d. 1893)
Bertha Benz [Cacilie Bertha Ringer], German inventor, automotive pioneer and wife of fellow automotive inventor Karl Benz, born in Pfrozheim, German Confederation (d. 1944)
Jacob Riis, Danish-American social reformer and "muck raking" reporter (NY Tribune, NY Evening Sun), born in Ribe, Denmark (d. 1914)
George Gore, American baseball player, born in Saccarappa, Maine (d. 1933)
Andy Adams, American writer (Log of a Cowboy), born in Indiana (d. 1935)
John Scott Haldane, Scottish physiologist, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1936)
Vito Volterra, Italian mathematician and physicist, born in Ancona, Italy (d. 1940)
Emmett Dalton, American outlaw of the Old West (The Dalton Gang), born in Belton, Missouri (d. 1937)
Jack Hearne, English cricket spin bowler (12 Tests, 49 wickets; England's first hat-trick vs Australia 1899), born in Chalfont St Giles, England (d. 1944)
Princess Helena Victoria of Schleswig-Holstein, granddaughter of Queen Victoria of the U.K., born in Frogmore House, Windsor, England (d. 1948)
Nicoline Magdalene Roll Anker, Norwegian author (Kvinnen og Den), born in Møre og Romsdal, Norway (d. 1942)
Pavlo Skoropadsky, Ukrainian politician and general, born in Wiesbaden, Germany (d. 1945)
François Coty, French perfume manufacturer, born in Ajaccio, Corsica, France (d. 1934)
V. Walfrid Ekman, Swedish oceanographer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1954)
Bert Hopkins, Australian cricket fast bowler (20 Tests, 26 wickets), born in Young, Australia (d. 1931)
E. B. Dwyer, Australian-born cricketer (Sussex), born in Redfern, Australia (d. 1912)
Karl Abraham, German psychoanalyst, born in Bremen, Germany (d. 1925)
Fergus McMaster, Australian businessman and aviation pioneer (Qantas), born in Morinish, Queensland (d. 1950)
Marcel Dupré, French organist and composer, born in Rouen, France (d. 1971)
Beulah Bondi, American actress (It's a Wonderful Life), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1981)
Alex Pompez, American Baseball HOF executive (owner NgL Cuban Stars [East] 1923-28; NY Cubans 1935-51), born in Key West, Florida (d. 1974)
Eppa Rixey Jr., American Baseball HOF pitcher (NL wins leader 1922; 266 career wins; Philadelphia Phillies, Cincinnati Reds), born in Culpeper, Virginia (d. 1963)
Tadeusz Peiper, Polish poet, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1969)
George Paget Thomson, English physicist who demonstrated electron diffraction (Nobel 1937), born in Cambridge, England (d. 1975)[1]
Hope Landin, American actress (I Remember Mama; Sugarfoot), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1973)
Konstantine Gamsakhurdia, Georgian writer and public benefactor, born in Abasha, Kutais Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1975)
Cornelius Van Til, Dutch philosopher and Christian apologist, born in Grootegast, Netherlands (d. 1987)
Ernst Kantorowicz, German-American historian (Laudes regiae), born in Posen, Prussia (d. 1963)
Karl Allmenröder, German World War I flying Ace, born in Wald, Rhine Province, Germany (d. 1917)
V. K. Krishna Menon, Indian nationalist, Minister of Defense (1957-62), born in Tellicherry, British India (d. 1974)
Septima Poinsette Clark, American educator and civil rights activist, born in CHarleston, South Carolina (d. 1987)
Aline MacMahon, American actress (Backdoor to Heaven), born in McKeesport, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
Gino Cervi, Italian actor (Les Miserables, Naked Maja), born in Bologna, Emilia-Romagna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1974)
Alfred Kastler, French physicist (Nobel 1966 - Hertzian resonances within atoms), born in Guebwiller, Alsace, German Empire (d. 1984)
Hugo Friedhofer, American cellist, orchestrator, and Academy Award-winning film composer (The Best Years of Our Lives; An Affair to Remember), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1981)
Jack La Rue, American actor and TV narrator (Lights Out, Mouthpiece, My Favorite Brunette), born in New York City (d. 1984)
Seton I. Miller, Hollywood screenwriter (Pete's Dragon, Istanbul), born in Chehalis, Washington (d. 1974)
Walter Slezak, Austrian-American stage and screen actor (Bedtime for Bonzo; Lifeboat; The Inspector General), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1983)
John Breeden, American actor (Salute, Madame Racketeer, Joy Street), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1977)
Albrecht Luitpold Ferdinand Michael, duke of Bavaria, born in Munich, German Empire (d. 1996)
Earl Wilson, American journalist and columnist (Midnight Earl), born in Rockford, Ohio (d. 1987)
Alceo Galliera, Italian conductor (Victoria Symphony; La Scala), and composer (The Wise Virgins and the Foolish Virgins), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1996)
Norman Corwin, American screenwriter for radio drama, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2011)
John Rhea "Yank" Lawson, American jazz trumpeter ("Dogtown Blues"; "Stormy Weather"), born in Trenton, Missouri (d. 1995)
Eleanore "May" Sarton, Belgian-American writer (Land of Silence), born in Wondelgem, Belgium (d. 1995)
Virgil Fox, American classical organist, born in Princeton, Illinois (d. 1980)
Earl Blackwell, American society impresario (Celebrity Register), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1995)
Heinz Kohut, Austrian-American psychoanalyst who opposed Freud and created the theory of self-psychology, born in Vienna, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1981)
William M. Inge, American playwright (Picnic-Pulitzer 1953), born in Independence, Kansas (d. 1973)
Evencio Castellanos, Venezuelan pianist, and composer, born in Cúa, Miranda state, Venezuela (d. 1984)
Stu Hart, Canadian professional wrestler and trainer, born in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada (d. 2003)
Henry B. González, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from Texas (1961-99), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 2000)
Léopold Simoneau, French Canadian tenor, born in Saint-Flavien, Quebec, Canada (d. 2006)
Pierre Emmanuel [Noël Mathieu], French poet (Sodome), born in Gan, Pyrénées-Atlantiques, France (d. 1984)
George Gaynes, Dutch-Finnish-American singer, stage and screen actor (Tootsie; Police Academy; General Hospital), born in Helsinki, Grand Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 2016)
James Penberthy, Australian composer, born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1999)
Kiro Gligorov, 1st President of the Republic of Macedonia (1991-99), born in Štip, Kingdom of Serbia (d. 2012)
Ted Bates, English soccer manager (Southampton 1955-73) and forward (Southampton	202 games), born in Thetford, England (d. 2003)
John Cullen Murphy, American comic strip artist, born in New York City (d. 2004)
John Lewis, American jazz pianist, arranger, and composer (Modern Jazz Quartet, 1952-97), born in La Grange, Illinois (d. 2001)
Nina Bara [Frances Bauer], Argentinian actress (Missile to the Moon, Space Patrol), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1990)
Joe Ames, American singer and actor (The Ames Brothers Show), born in Malden, Massachusetts (d. 2007)
Vasco Gonçalves, Portuguese army officer and 103rd Prime Minister of Portugal (1974-75), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 2005)
Len Shackleton, English former footballer, born in Bradford, England (d. 2000)
Marina Svetlova, American ballerina and choreographer (Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo), born in Paris (d. 2009)
George Hadjinikos, Greek pianist and conductor, born in Volos, Greece (d. 2015)
Ralph Hall, American politician, (oldest to serve in the House of Representatives, Texas 1981-2014), born in Fate, Texas (d. 2019)[1]
Jane Morgan [Florence Currier], American singer ("The Day The Rains Came"; "Fascination"; "If I Could Only Live My Life Again"), stage and television actress, born in Newton, Massachusetts (d. 2025)
Ken Tyrrell, English auto racer and founder of Tyrrell Racing, born in East Horsley, Surrey, England (d. 2001)
Mary Carver [Carvellas], American actress (Simon & Simon; Star Trek: Enterprise), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2013)
Ngiratkel Etpison, Palauan businessman and politician (President of Palau, 1989-92), born in Koror, South Seas Mandate (d. 1997)
Tom O'Horgan, American musical theater director (Hair; Jesus Christ Superstar), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2009)
Yehuda Amichai, Israeli poet (Now and In Other Days), born in Würzburg, Germany (d. 2000)
Ann B. Davis, American actress (The Bob Cummings Show, The Brady Bunch), born in Schenectady, New York (d. 2014)
Dave Dudley, American country singer ("Six Days on the Road"), born in Spencer, Wisconsin (d. 2003)
Jeanne Bal, American actress (Pat-Love & Marriage, Star Trek- The Man Trap), born in Santa Monica, California (d. 1996)
Jahanara Imam, Bangladeshi writer and political activist, born in Murshidabad, West Bengal, India (d. 1994)
Juan Gelman, Argentine poet, born in Buenos Aires (d. 2014)
Joe Layton, American director and choreographer, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1994)
